coredata.dc:title: Waste to value transformation: Converting Carica papaya seeds into green fluorescent carbon dots for simultaneous selective detection and degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride in water
coredata.dc:creator: Laddha, Harshita
Yadav, Priya
Sharma, Manish
Agarwal, Madhu
Gupta, Ragini
coredata.dc:description: Rampant use of antibiotics has resulted in their seepage into groundwater and ultimately ending up in the food chain, causing antimicrobial resistance. To address this issue, it is imperative to not only quantitatively detect but eliminate them from water. An eco-friendly, one-step microwave-induced pyrolysis of waste papaya seeds (PS) with ethylenediamine (EDA) for just 5min gave green fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (PS-CDs), which are capable of detecting and photocatalytically degrading TC. The fluorescence properties of PS-CDs displayed that it has high sensitivity and selectivity towards sensing of TC with a detection limit as low as 120 nM. Also, the method gave satisfactory recovery results when extrapolated to determine TC in spiked milk, orange juice, tap water, and honey samples. On the other hand, PS-CDs alone potentially function as an efficient photocatalyst for the degradation of TC. PS-CDs' dual functionality provides an effectual method for the simultaneous detection and degradation of TC by a single nanoprobe.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rampant use of antibiotics has resulted in their seepage into groundwater and ultimately ending up in the food chain, causing antimicrobial resistance. To address this issue, it is imperative to not only quantitatively detect but eliminate them from water. An eco-friendly, one-step microwave-induced pyrolysis of waste papaya seeds (PS) with ethylenediamine (EDA) for just 5min gave green fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (PS-CDs), which are capable of detecting and photocatalytically degrading TC. The fluorescence properties of PS-CDs displayed that it has high sensitivity and selectivity towards sensing of TC with a detection limit as low as 120 nM. Also, the method gave satisfactory recovery results when extrapolated to determine TC in spiked milk, orange juice, tap water, and honey samples. On the other hand, PS-CDs alone potentially function as an efficient photocatalyst for the degradation of TC. PS-CDs' dual functionality provides an effectual method for the simultaneous detection and degradation of TC by a single nanoprobe.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline, a broad-spectrum antibiotic developed in the mid-20th century (Bahrami et al., 2012), has been used globally to treat and prevent microbial diseases such as giardiasis, filarial worms, rosacea, digital dermatitis, and others in veterinary and human medicine (Jiang and Dai, 2023; Zhou et al., 2017). TC also promotes growth in cattle, animals, and fisheries, increasing average body weight by 4 – 5 % compared to controls (Bahrami et al., 2012; Conzuelo et al., 2012). Huang et al. reported that TC is universally used to treat mastitis in dairy animals during lactation, but only 25 – 50 % of its dosage is metabolized (Muhammad et al., 2020). A significant fraction of tetracycline is excreted into groundwater, soil, and surface water as the parent compound or its metabolites through urine and faeces (Charuaud et al., 2019; Chopra and Roberts, 2001; Fu et al., 2021). Its widespread and unjustified use to treat microbial diseases has led to antibiotic resistance in human pathogens and severe consequences (Dong et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2009). Antibiotic residues in meat, eggs, milk, fish, and honey are highly toxic and damaging, causing gastrointestinal disturbance, tooth yellowing, allergies, nephrotoxicity, and blood or central nervous system reactions (Granados-Chinchilla and Rodríguez, 2017; Kurittu et al., 2000; Kushikawa et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2023). For these reasons, several regulatory agencies, including the European Union (EU) and the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA), have set the maximum residual limits in animal-origin foodstuffs to 225 nM and 676 nM, respectively (Yang et al., 2020a). Considering the severity of the problem, a number of techniques for detecting and removing contaminants have been developed, but the majority of them can only be utilized for one single function, i.e., either to detect (Liu et al., 2018; Xing et al., 2020) or to remove (Leichtweis et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2021) TC individually. However, in real implementations, contamination detection and removal should complement each other. As a result, it would be tempting to create an integrated system capable of simultaneously detecting and removing TC, as eliminating pollutants from the environment is as essential as detecting contaminants analytically.

In this scenario, carbon dots (CDs), as the newest member of the carbon nanoparticle family, have gained momentum due to their unique optical, thermal, and electronic properties like promising photoluminescence, superior biocompatibility, low toxicity (He et al., 2022; Sohal et al., 2021), etc. which resulted in their extensive use in diverse applications, viz. light-emitting diode (Zhao and Tan, 2021), anti-counterfeiting (Kalytchuk et al., 2018), photocatalysis (Laddha et al., 2022a), energy conversion/storage devices (Xiao et al., 2021), information encryption (Muthamma et al., 2021), artificial nose (Shauloff et al., 2021), sensing (Kainth et al., 2022; Laddha et al., 2022b), bioimaging (Boakye-Yiadom et al., 2019), and drug delivery (Zeng et al., 2016). Because of their steady and tunable emission characteristics, CDs are defined as a fascinating environmentally friendly fluorescence probe for detecting a wide variety of target analytes (Sohal et al., 2020). Fluorescence-based sensors have shown a high level of appealing performance in terms of ease of operation, rapid response, low detection limit, little sample dosage requirement, good selectivity, and cheap cost of analysis; thus, they have been effectively utilized for sensing of analytes (Huang et al., 2022). Moreover, CDs also possess a superior ability to facilitate the recombination annihilation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the effective separation of photogenerated excitons (Laddha et al., 2022a). Combining the dual properties of fluorescence and catalytic capability, it was believed that carbon dots would be promising as the multifunctional nanomaterials for simultaneous detection and catalytic degradation of pollutants, which was anticipated to meet the significant challenge for the integration of visually in-situ monitoring and synchronously catalysing the degradation of pollutants (Dhenadhayalan et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2022). A variety of methods such as pyrolysis, hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment, laser ablation, plasma irradiation, microwave irradiation, chemical ablation, and electrochemical carbonization (Liu et al., 2020; Sharma and Das, 2019; Xiao et al., 2021), have been employed for the synthesis of carbon dots using several materials such as graphite (Wang et al., 2011), activated carbon (Qiao et al., 2010), glucose (Papaioannou et al., 2018), citric acid (Laddha et al., 2022a), etc. Though, the most recent trend for synthesizing CDs circumambulates around the usage of natural and residual resources as precursors in an attempt to reduce the environmental burden of the synthesis (Wareing et al., 2021). For instance, Feng et al. reported the synthesis of N-doped CDs from strawberries with a quantum yield of 6.3 % using the hydrothermal method and presented their application for the sensing of mercury ions (Huang et al., 2013). Atchudan et al. used Chionanthus retusus fruit for synthesizing N-doped carbon dots with a 9 % quantum yield. They employed these CDs as a biological probe for the detection of ferric ions (Atchudan et al., 2017). However, exploiting food directly for synthesizing carbon dots also has certain limitations, as in numerous parts of the world, there is a shortage of food, and it is profoundly unsatisfactory to squander edible materials for the synthesis of carbon dots (de Oliveira and da Silva Abreu, 2021; Wareing et al., 2021). In this direction, the utilization of waste seeds of fruits as a precursor for the preparation of carbon dots is highly desirable (Kaur and Verma, 2022). Thus, turning waste seeds into valuable products could have significant economic and environmental benefits.

Hereby, a simple and efficient method to synthesize CDs has been built up by using waste papaya seeds (Carica papaya) as a carbon source and EDA as a nitrogen dopant (Scheme 1 ). Analyses were conducted to characterize the morphological, microstructural, and physicochemical properties of PS-CDs. Due to the presence of unique optical and electrical properties, PS-CDs act as a potential “two-in-one” probe for simultaneous detection as well as degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride. The influence of different factors (pH, concentration, interfering compounds, etc.) on fluorescence sensing and degradation of TC by PS-CDs were discussed in detail. This study enlightens the new concepts and strategies for the creation of multifunctional nanomaterials for detecting and eliminating toxic chemical contaminants.

For all the experiments, analytically pure chemicals were utilized, which needed no further purification. All the syntheses and sensing studies were carried out with deionized (DI) water. Papaya was brought from a local fruit market (Jaipur, Rajasthan, India).

For the synthesis of the carbon dots, Samsung Combi (CE104VD) microwave oven (household) was utilized. For centrifugation and ultrasonication, Remi R–12C Plus and Elma S 70 H Ultrasonicator (37 kHz) were used, respectively. HI 3221 pH meter, Hanna Instruments, was used for adjusting the pH values of the samples. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed to examine the size and shape of PS-CDs using a Tecnai 20 G2 (300 kV) STWIN model. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analyses were performed using ESCA + omicron nanotechnology, Oxford Instrument, and Bruker FTIR spectrophotometer (Alpha; range 500 – 4000 cm−1) to investigate their chemical composition and functional groups, respectively. The fluorescence (FL) and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were analyzed on Fluoromax 4C.L spectrophotometer and Shimadzu UV-1800 Spectrophotometer, respectively. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis was done on Agilent 1260 Infinity II (G7129A), Germany model using methanol as mobile phase. The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and high resolution mass spectra (HRMS) in the electrospray ionization (ESI) mode. were recorded by Jeol ECS 400 multinuclear spectrophotometer and Xevo G2-S Q-Tof mass spectrometer (Waters). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were performed on K-Lyte 1.2 Potentiostat utilizing glassy carbon as a working electrode, Pt wire as a counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode at 50 mV s−1. TOC was measured using TOC-L total carbon analyzer from Shimadzu Corporation, Japan.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of waste papaya seeds derived carbon dots (PS-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A one-step microwave-induced pyrolysis method was utilized to synthesize PS-CDs (Laddha et al., 2022b). Carica papaya seeds were collected from the house waste, washed thoroughly, and dried in sunlight. Firstly, 0.5 g of dried papaya seeds were crushed in a mortar pestle and screened through a standard sieve of 80 mesh size to obtain a fine powder. Then seed powder was sonicated for 15 min in a conical flask with a 5 mL mixture of deionized water and ethylene diamine (4:1), resulting in a whitish-colored suspension. Afterward, the conical flask containing suspension was placed in the household microwave oven for 5 min at 600 W (heating intermittently for 2.5 min and taking a break of 20 sec in between). After 5 min of the reaction, most of the liquid mixture evaporated, and the whitish suspension turned brownish, depicting the charring of the reactants. The charred mixture was then allowed to cool at ambient temperature. It was further diluted with deionized water (10 mL) and centrifuged at 13000 rpm for 30 min. After being filtered through a 0.22 mm syringe filter, the solution was dialyzed for 24 h against water. Finally, the PS-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator. The volume ratio of water: EDA mixture was added to the fixed amount of papaya seeds to optimize against quantum yield (Fig. S1).

The detailed procedure for the quantum yield measurements, sensing of TC, analysis of TC in real samples and tablets, and photocatalytic degradation studies of TC were provided in Supplementary Information (Text S1–S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, microstructural, and optical characterization of PS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size and morphological characteristics of PS-CDs were explored using HRTEM, which showed they are evenly scattered and spherical in shape, as displayed in Fig. 1 (a, b) at different magnifications. Fig. 1 (c) shows the size distribution histogram of the PS-CDs with particle sizes ranging from 2 to 6.5 nm, with an average size of approximately 4.68 nm.

The amount of nitrogen doping and surficial states of the PS-CDs were examined using the XPS. Fig. 2 (a) depicted the survey spectrum of PS-CDs exhibiting three peaks at 285.6, 400.5, and 533.0 eV related to C1s (72.15 %), N1s (10.48 %), and O1s (17.36 %), respectively with their percent elemental composition (Sharma et al., 2022b) (Laddha et al., 2022a). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2 (b)) was deconvoluted into four peaks at 283.7 eV, 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV, and 287.1 eV consisting of C=C, C–C, C–N/C–O, and C=O/C=N bonds, respectively. Fig. 2 (c) displayed the deconvoluted peaks at 398.5 eV, 399.3 eV, and 400.7 eV attributed to pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic bonded nitrogen, respectively in the high-resolution spectrum of N1s. In the high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2 (d)), the two prominent bands located at 531.5 eV and 530.5 eV correspond to the C–O and C=O bindings, respectively (Kainth et al., 2020; Sharma et al., 2022a).

FTIR analysis also provided insight about the doping and functional groups present on PS-CDs. The comparative FTIR analyses of PS-CDs, papaya seeds and EDA was done to confirm the formation of PS-CDs (Fig. S2). The merged and broad peaks located at 3401 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of PS-CDs (Fig. 3 (a)) are associated with –OH or/and –NH stretching vibrations (Laddha et al., 2022a). A small peak at 2925 cm−1 corresponds to the =C–H stretching vibration. The prominent absorption band at 1596 cm−1 is ascribed to the –NH bending vibrations. The emergence of peaks at 1479 cm−1 and 1326 cm−1 are due to C–H bending and –C–N stretching vibrations, respectively. The absorption band at 1100 cm−1 is attributed to C–O and C–O–C stretching vibrations (Ren et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020b). The results from XPS and FTIR analyses revealed that PS-CDs have abundant hydrophilic functional groups and good water solubility, making them potential candidates for future application investigations.

PS-CDs exhibit a very less intense peak at 268 nm and a hump at 350 nm with a tail extending into the visible range. These absorption bands in the UV–visible region are ascribed to π–π* and n–π* transitions of C=C and C=O/C=N groups, respectively (Fig. 3 (b)) (Kaur et al., 2019; Yadav et al., 2021a, 2022a). PS-CDs are uniformly dispersed in the water and showed pale yellow color in ambient light and green-colored luminescence under UV light illumination (Fig. 3 (b)-inset). Fig. 3 (c) displays the excitation wavelength-dependent emission spectrum of PS-CDs with the red shift in the emission band on exciting from 280 nm to 500 nm with a maximum emission band at 499 nm with 400 nm excitation wavelength (λ max). The fluorescence QY of PS-CDs was determined to be 10.05 % in water w.r.t. quinine sulphate as a reference (Crosby and Demas, 1971).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and stability of PS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH ranging from 2 to 12 was evaluated from emission spectra of PS-CDs. Fig. S3 revealed that PS-CDs exhibit maximum fluorescence at pH 7. However, a gradual decrease was noticed in acidic and basic media. This decrement could be attributed to the dominance of easily ionisable –NH2 and –COOH groups on the surface of PS-CDs compared to –OH and C=O groups, resulting into their protonation and deprotonation at extreme pH conditions (Ren et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020b).

The stability of the PS-CDs in terms of storage, UV irradiation, ionic conditions, and the temperature was examined for their applicability as a fluorescent probe in various fields. Fig. S4 (a) displayed the fluorescence emission spectra of PS-CDs solution at different storage times from 1 to 120 days. No appreciable change in FL intensity was observed even after 120 days, which confirms the prolonged stability of PS-CDs. Moreover, the solution of PS-CDs showed negligible change in the FL intensity after UV light irradiation (365 nm) for a continuous 90 min suggesting the remarkable photostability of the PS-CDs (Fig. S4 (b)). The emission intensity of the fluorescent PS-CDs is relatively constant throughout the range of NaCl solutions from 0 to 1.0 M, demonstrating the ionic stability of PS-CDs even in high sodium environments (Fig. S5 (a)) (Song et al., 2016). Moreover, the effect of various ions (0.5 M), including Ca2+, HPO4 −, K+, Cl− and NH4 + on the fluorescence intensity of PS-CDs was also investigated (Fig. S14), which also shows the minimal influence on it, suggesting stable fluorescence of PS-CDs (Song et al., 2016). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity was stable over the temperature ranging from 5 to 65 °C, implying the admirable stability of PS-CDs (Fig. S5 (b)). Thus, PS-CDs have good time, photo, ionic and thermal stability, making them an effective luminescent probe that could be valuable for various analytical and microscopic purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing studies of PS-CDs with TC
Fluorescence spectroscopic studies of PS-CDs with antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para:
The analytical applicability of PS-CDs has been examined in the search for possible applications as sensors. PS-CDs alone exhibit a strong emission band centered at 499 nm when excited at 400 nm. The fluorescence behavior of the PS-CDs after the addition of different types of antibiotics (5 × 10−4 M), viz. chloramphenicol (CH), amoxicillin (AM), streptomycin (ST), cephalexin (CE), norfloxacin (NO) except, tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) showed no substantial alteration in fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4
(a)). However, the addition of TC selectively and drastically quenched the FL spectra of PS-CDs at 499 nm, which may be defined with the aid of inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. The IFE could have resulted from the overlap of the absorption band of tetracycline on the excitation and/or emission band of PS-CDs (Fig. 6 (b)). The figure shows a spectral overlap between the absorption band of the absorber (TC) and excitation band of the fluorophore (PS-CDs). Hence, due to the inner filter effect, TC can conceal appropriate excitation of PS-CDs and diminish emission of PS-CDs. In addition, Fig. 4 (c) displayed a digital picture of PS-CDs exposed under UV light at 365 nm revealing substantial fluorescence quenching of PS-CDs with TC.

To determine the fluorescence quenching capability of the PS-CDs toward TC detection, FL titration was performed with various concentrations of TC ranging from 0 to 550 μM. As depicted in Fig. 4 (b), a progressive increment in TC concentration resulted in the gradual reduction in the FL intensity at 499 nm (λ max = 400 nm), reaching a maximum limit at 550 μM beyond which no drop in the FL intensity was noted. The plot between Fo/F vs. concentration of TC showed two linear relationships ranging from 0.1 to 20 μM (R2 = 0.9927) and 25 to 250 μM (R2 = 0.9987) (Fig. 5 (b)) by following the Stern-Volmer equation as given below (Laddha et al., 2022a):

(where K sv is the slope of the Stern-Volmer calibration plot; Fo and F are the FL intensity of PS-CDs before and after the addition of tetracycline, respectively). Moreover, the detection limit was calculated to be 0.12 μM (120 nM) for TC by following formula 3(σ)/Ksv (where σ is the standard deviation) (Huang et al., 2013; Yadav et al., 2022b). The limit of detection is analogous to the previously published reports on fluorescent carbon dots demonstrating improved sensitivity of PS-CDs towards TC (Table S1).

To ascertain the selectivity and specificity of PS-CDs for TC detection, the competitive interference study was conducted with various antibiotics and metal ions (5 × 10−4 M), comprising CH, AM, ST, CE, NO, Na (I), Cu (II), Pb (II), Ca (II), Zn (II), Al (III), Hg (II), Co (II), Mg (II), and Fe (III) in water at pH 7 at ambient temperature (Fig. 5 (a)). The obtained results showed that no momentous change in the fluorescence intensity of the PS-CDs and PS-CDs + TC was seen at 499 nm on excitation at 400 nm with other interfering antibiotics and metal ions. The investigation demonstrated that the presence of other competing antibiotics/metal ions did not interfere with the recognition of TC; consequently, PS-CDs may be reliably used as a selective sensor for TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the sensing of TC by PS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characteristics of both PS-CDs and TC were explored to investigate the mechanism behind the detection of TC by fluorescence quenching of PS-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 (b), the absorption spectra of TC at 360 nm coincide with the excitation spectrum of PS-CDs at 400 nm, demonstrating that TC may quench PS-CD fluorescence via the inner filter effect.

In addition, to confirm the existence or non-existence of electron transfer process, the calculation was done for the determination of energy levels of PS-CDs, i.e., highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), applying the following formula (Sharma and Yun, 2020; Zhang et al., 2014):

The cyclic voltammetric graph of the PS-CDs was obtained in the aqueous condition as depicted in Fig. 6 (c), through which the reduction potential (E red) of PS-CDs was determined and found to be – 0.25 eV. Then, the calculation was done for determining E LUMO and found to be – 4.11 eV according to Eq. (4). Finally, the calculations for obtaining the values for the energy level E HOMO, E g, and E red was carried out by following the equation (Liu et al., 2017; Yan et al., 2019):
(4)

The optical bandgap (E g) was determined to be 5.25 eV from the UV-absorption spectrum of the PS-CDs by extrapolating the absorption edge of the curve involving (αhν)2 and hν when α = 0 (Fig. S6); thus, E HOMO value was found to be – 9.36 eV. E HOMO and E LUMO of tetracycline were determined to be 6.2 eV and 3.87 eV, respectively, calculated using the B3LYP method in the Dmol3 module using the Materials Studio (version 8.0) program suite (Yadav et al., 2021b). As represented in Fig. 6 (d), the photo-excited electrons of the PS-CDs cannot be transferred to the LUMO energy level of tetracycline as the LUMO of tetracycline is comparatively higher than that of PS-CDs (Yan et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). Therefore, the possibility of photo-induced electron transfer (PET) among PS-CDs and tetracycline is negligible. Lastly, the FTIR spectra of PS-CDs were analyzed before and after the addition of TC. The experimental results (Fig. S7) revealed no substantial shift in the absorption peaks of PS-CDs after adding TC. Furthermore, the addition of tetracycline did not result in the formation of new peaks in the FTIR spectra of PS-CDs + TC, implying that no new functionality was developed, suggesting an IFE mechanism for detecting TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in water, food samples, and tablets
ce:section.ce:para: In order to validate the feasibility of the proposed method, tap water, milk, orange juice, honey samples, and commercial tablets were utilized to assess the dependability and efficiency of this fluorescent nanoprobe. The samples of tap water, milk, orange juice, and honey were pre-treated and spiked with particular amounts of TC, and the analysis findings are shown in Table 1 . Spiked recoveries ranged from 88 to 106.8 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 7.41 %. The outcomes were consistent with those obtained using the conventional UV spectroscopy technique for detecting TC (Anand et al., 2019; Li et al., 2022), indicating that the fluorescent probe developed was liable for TC. These results proposed that the presented method has impressive potential for determining TC in water and food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation studies of TC with PS-CDs
Photocatalytic degradation studies of TC with PS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic degradation of TC (25 ppm) by PS-CDs was examined through UV–vis spectroscopy at a wavelength of 370 nm (Fig. S8). The relative change in concentration of TC (C/Co) with time in conjunction with a considerable fluctuation in the degradation rate was measured in order to track the photocatalytic degradation of TC. Fig. 7 (a) displays the effect of PS-CDs on the degradation of 25 ppm TC solution in the absence and presence of sunlight with and without photocatalyst. The graph clearly demonstrates that although the photocatalyst has little effect in the dark (3 % TC was adsorbed), it rapidly degrades 90 % of the TC (25 ppm) after being exposed to solar light for only 22 min. In order to examine the impact of varied pH on TC degradation, a series of TC degradation performances were assessed at pH 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11. PS-CDs had strong catalytic activity throughout a broad pH range, as shown in Fig. S16. The photocatalytic degradation of TC by PS-CDs hardly fluctuated with different pH values, indicating the minimal impact of the pH on the photodegradation efficiency (Sun et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2022). Hence, neutral pH was considered an optimum condition, and all the experiments were carried out at the same. The rate of photocatalytic degradation is determined by fitting the experimental data into the following pseudo-first-order kinetic model, which may be stated as Eq. (5) given below (Behnajady et al., 2006):
(5)
ln
(
C o
/ C
)
= k t
Where Co (ppm) and C (ppm) indicates the concentration of TC initially and at time (t). k (min−1) is the reaction rate constant calculated from the slope of the curve.

As demonstrated in Fig. 7 (b), the experimental data are well linearly fitted to the pseudo-first-order kinetics (r2 = 0.987). The rate constant (k, min−1) and half-life (t1/2, min) was determined to be 0.0775 min−1 and 0.8931 min, respectively for TC degradation (25 ppm) from kinetics plot. HPLC analyses also confirmed the photocatalytic degradation of TC by PS-CDs (Fig. S9). Control TC has a sharp peak at the retention time of 5.54 min; however, the diminished peak was observed in the degraded TC sample after 22 min of solar irradiation suggesting degradation of TC.

Furthermore, to determine the active radical species responsible for the photocatalytic degradation of TC by PS-CDs, a few trapping tests were conducted using several scavengers viz. di-sodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Na2EDTA) complex for holes (h+), tertiary butyl alcohol (t-BuOH) used for hydroxyl radicals (OH•), and p-benzoquinone (BZQ) meant for superoxide radicals (O2 •–) (Huang et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2020). Fig. 7 (c) displays that the photodegradation rate reduces up to 12 %, 27 %, and 76 % in the presence of Na2EDTA, t-BuOH, and BZQ, respectively, suggesting that O2 •– radicals are mainly responsible for the photodegradation of TC. However, the effect of trapping agent Na2EDTA and t-BuOH for holes and OH• radicals is very less pronounced on the photodegradation compared to BZQ. In accordance with the results of the trapping experiment, the plausible mechanism involved in the photodegradation process is displayed in Fig. 7 (d). Additionally, the production of superoxide radical (O2 •–) from PS-CDs under sunlight has also been confirmed using the nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT) test, Fig. S15 (Dalal et al., 2023). The graph shows that the absorbance at 259 nm wavelength decreases significantly in the presence of PS-CDs within 22 min under sunlight, suggesting the generation of superoxide radicals (O2 •–).

Moreover, HRMS spectra were also recorded at varying time intervals during the photocatalytic degradation process to examine the plausible degradation pathway and products (Fig. S10). Mass spectra recorded showed the degradation of TC (m/z = 445) into smaller hydrocarbons when exposed to sunlight with PS-CDs (Fig. S11) (Borghi and Palma, 2014; Shi et al., 2021). The possible TC degradation pathway was hypothesized by combining the intermediate products detected on mass spectrometry and previous findings (Zhu et al., 2022). Initially, the TC molecule was degraded through a series of loss of N-methyl and amino group due to the weak bond energy of N–C bond resulting in the intermediate product (m/z = 418) (Sun et al., 2022). Thereafter, the additional opening of benzene rings with fall-off functional groups (acids and alcohols) on the carbocycle proceeded, eventually degrading to m/z = 284 and 277 (Zhu et al., 2022). Ultimately, these cyclic intermediates may transform into short-chain compounds, H2O and CO2.

Futhermore, the mineralization of the TC was evaluated by total organic carbon measurements. Fig. S13 illustrates the TOC removal percentages for TC with time. The removal percentage of TC and TOC increases with time due to the generation of free reactive radicals that degrades TC during sunlight irradiation. The TOC removal percentage of TC after 22 min was found to be 51.66 % (Huang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NMR & FTIR analyses for the photocatalytic degradation process
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation of TC by PS-CDs was also validated using nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analyses. The samples (control TC and degraded TC) for the proton NMR (1H NMR) analysis were collected before and after the photocatalytic degradation, dried at ∼60 °C, and stored under vacuum. Fig. 8 presents the 1H NMR spectra of control TC compared with the photodegraded TC sample recorded in D2O solvent. The 1H NMR spectra (pink line) of control TC show peaks in the aromatic (δ 7.0 − 7.6 ppm) region as well as in the aliphatic (δ 2.5 − 4.85 ppm) region. However, the distinctive peaks of the control TC disappeared from the aromatic and aliphatic regions in the 1H NMR spectra after photocatalytic degradation of the TC molecules (purple line), indicating the breakage of the TC's stable organic configuration. In addition, the appearance of small peaks in the aliphatic area implies the production of low molecular masses hydrocarbons during the photodegradation process (Bhati et al., 2018).

Moreover, a comparative FTIR examination of TC and TC + PS-CDs (22 min) also supported the NMR results verifying photocatalytic degradation of TC (Fig. S12). FTIR spectrum of the control TC sample showed the broad and merged stretching vibrational peak at 3405 cm−1 for N–H and O–H bonds. The absorption peaks at 2917 - 2845 cm−1 and 1618 - 1592 cm−1 were attributed to CH3 and C=C stretching vibrations, respectively. Aromatic C–H bending appeared at 1460 cm−1 and CH3 bending at 1398 cm−1. The FTIR data of the control sample was well supported by the reported data (Trivedi et al., 2015). However, the characteristic peaks of TC diminish after 22 min of sunlight irradiation, and only peaks corresponding to PS-CDs were seen in the FTIR spectra of the photodegraded TC sample. The disappearance of peaks signified the photodegradation of TC after 22 min of sunlight irradiation with PS-CDs.

In conclusion, a sensitive and selective waste-to-value approach has been reported for assaying and detecting tetracycline in water and food samples. Green luminous nitrogen-doped carbon dots having a quantum yield of 10.05 % were prepared by simple microwave-induced pyrolysis of waste papaya seeds and EDA. PS-CDs have a proficient ability to selectively detect TC at 499 nm when excited at 400 nm via an inner filter effect with a detection limit as low as 120 nM. Other antibiotics, such as chloramphenicol, amoxicillin, streptomycin, cephalexin, and norfloxacin, had no discernible effect on PS-CDs. Additionally, the fluorescent sensor was used to quantify TC in milk, orange juice, tap water, honey samples, and TC tablets, with 88 – 106.8 % recoveries. Furthermore, the photocatalytic degradation studies confirmed the 90 % degradation of TC with PS-CDs. The proposed work offers a simple and quick strategy for detecting and degrading tetracycline in water. Thus, employing fluorescent carbon dots developed using waste seeds of papaya provides a sustainable and innovative two-edged probe for the simultaneous sensing and remediation of organic pollutants.

Harshita Laddha: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing-Original draft preparation, Priya Yadav: Investigation, Visualization, Writing-Original draft preparation, Manish Sharma: Investigation, Visualization, Madhu Agarwal: Supervision, Visualization, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Ragini Gupta: Conceptualization, Supervision, Visualization, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.
